# Soil Carbon Dynamics in Alpine Ecosystems: Evidence from the Swiss National Park

**Authors:** A. Rodríguez, M. Kovač, J. Lindström
**Version:** 0.1 — for internal review
**Date:** 2026-05-01

---

## Abstract

Alpine soils store approximately 15% of terrestrial carbon in less than 3% of global land area. We measured soil organic carbon, moisture, and temperature at ten high-elevation sites in the Swiss National Park during the 2026 spring snowmelt campaign. Carbon efflux peaked 6.2 ± 1.8 days after peak soil moisture (r² = 0.74). Current land-surface models underestimate spring emissions by 18–24%. Incorporating an explicit lag term reduces RMSE by 31%.

---

## Data Availability Statement

The data supporting this study's findings are openly available in EnviDat at https://doi.org/10.16904/envidat.XXXX. The dataset includes soil chemistry measurements, temperature and humidity sensor data, and R analysis scripts from alpine sites in the Swiss National Park (2026) and is available under a CC BY 4.0 license.

---

## 5. Conclusions

Alpine soils exhibit a reproducible 6–8 day lag between peak snowmelt soil moisture and peak CO₂ efflux. This lag should be incorporated into land-surface models to reduce spring-season carbon budget bias.
